title: Editor and terminal workflow

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Editor and terminal workflow; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Editor and terminal workflow
sources.claim: An editor changes source files, a terminal runs commands in a working directory, and saving the file is a separate action from executing a named tool on it.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Engineering; August 2023; section applicable to Editor and terminal workflow
sources.claim: An editor changes source files, a terminal runs commands in a working directory, and saving the file is a separate action from executing a named tool on it.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An editor changes source files, a terminal runs commands in a working directory, and saving the file is a separate action from executing a named tool on it.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner saves main.py, confirms the terminal is in the project folder, runs python main.py, and distinguishes program output from source text. Your goal is to use a terminal simulator to navigate a project and run a named tool. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Source-to-execution models; Examples and edge cases; Files, folders and paths. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: An editor changes source files, a terminal runs commands in a working directory, and saving the file is a separate action from executing a named tool on it. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Shell syntax, PATH lookup, quoting, environment variables, permissions, and editor autosave behavior differ, so commands and active files must be verified explicitly. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Typing code in an editor automatically runs it, and every terminal starts in the folder containing the visible file.

includes: Edit, save, navigate and run a supplied command without confusing source and output.
Use a terminal simulator to navigate a project and run a named tool.
excludes: Shell syntax, PATH lookup, quoting, environment variables, permissions, and editor autosave behavior differ, so commands and active files must be verified explicitly.

outcomes: Use a terminal simulator to navigate a project and run a named tool.

examples.title: Editor and terminal workflow: direct case
examples.situation: A learner saves main.py, confirms the terminal is in the project folder, runs python main.py, and distinguishes program output from source text.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A learner saves main.py, confirms the terminal is in the project folder, runs python main.py, and distinguishes program output from source text.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Save intentionally, inspect the working directory and command, run the declared tool, and read its output or diagnostic as a separate workflow step.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Save intentionally, inspect the working directory and command, run the declared tool, and read its output or diagnostic as a separate workflow step.

examples.title: Editor and terminal workflow: boundary case
examples.situation: A command fails because the terminal remains in the parent folder; printing the working directory reveals the mismatch before any code change.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A command fails because the terminal remains in the parent folder; printing the working directory reveals the mismatch before any code change.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Shell syntax, PATH lookup, quoting, environment variables, permissions, and editor autosave behavior differ, so commands and active files must be verified explicitly.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Typing code in an editor automatically runs it, and every terminal starts in the folder containing the visible file.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Use a terminal simulator to navigate a project and run a named tool.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Typing code in an editor automatically runs it, and every terminal starts in the folder containing the visible file.
misconceptions.correction: Save intentionally, inspect the working directory and command, run the declared tool, and read its output or diagnostic as a separate workflow step.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Typing code in an editor automatically runs it, and every terminal starts in the folder containing the visible file.

analogy.example: A repair bench can model Editor and terminal workflow: identify the correct part, use a named tool, read the first reliable symptom, test one cause, and verify the repair.
usefulFor: Building a careful inspect, edit, run, diagnose, and verify workflow
limitations: Software tools have shell parsing, filesystem, runtime, and diagnostic rules that physical tools do not share.
Shell syntax, PATH lookup, quoting, environment variables, permissions, and editor autosave behavior differ, so commands and active files must be verified explicitly.

recap.summary: You should now be able to use a terminal simulator to navigate a project and run a named tool. Keep this boundary in view: Shell syntax, PATH lookup, quoting, environment variables, permissions, and editor autosave behavior differ, so commands and active files must be verified explicitly.
